meta_title: ScamGuard — alerts about phone scammers in Moldova
meta_description: Free Android app that monitors for signs of phone fraud and warns you. Works locally, no data transmission.
hero_subtitle: Free Android app for Moldova residents. Monitors for fraud signals and warns you when a scammer calls and asks for an SMS code.
how_subtitle: Three steps — and the app runs in background 24/7
step1_desc: Download the APK and install. Free, no registration, no ads.
step2_desc: One-time setup — the app will request necessary permissions and show you how to grant them.
step3_desc: ScamGuard runs in background. If you receive a code during a suspicious call — you'll see a warning.
features_subtitle: Simple and clear feature set without extras
f1_desc: The app detects when you receive a one-time confirmation code and correlates it with an active call.
f2_desc: Alert triggers only if the code arrived during or right after a call. Regular SMS codes don't bother you.
f3_desc: Recognizes SMS from MAIB, Victoriabank, MICB, Orange, Moldcell and others — shows who sent it.
f4_desc: Large red button in the alert ends the scammer's call immediately — no need to dig through menus.
f5_desc: All SMS processing happens locally on the device. Internet is only needed for update checks.
f6_desc: English, Russian and Romanian. Switched with one button in settings.
privacy_subtitle: We understand that an app working with SMS requires special trust
p1_desc: SMS analysis, history, contacts — everything stays only on your device. Nothing goes to the server.
p2_desc: Your SMS content never leaves the phone. The app technically has nothing to send — we have no endpoint to receive SMS.
p3_desc: The only network request — an anonymous hash on first launch confirming you accepted the terms of use.
screens_subtitle: Simple interface — even for those uncomfortable with tech
screen1_desc: You can see that the app is working. Safety tips are always visible.
screen2_desc: Loud visual warning at the moment of danger. One button — and the call is ended.
faq_a1: ScamGuard recognizes a common phone scam pattern: a call combined with a confirmation code arriving by SMS. When it detects this combination, the app shows a warning — don't share the code with anyone, and call back only using the official number. All messages are processed only on your device, nothing is sent anywhere.
faq_q2: What should I do if I suspect a scammer?
faq_a2: Hang up immediately — don't continue the conversation and don't share any codes, passwords, or card details. Then call the organization back yourself, using the official number — not the one the caller gave you, but one from the organization's website, the back of your card, or the
button in a ScamGuard warning. Never call back a number left by a suspicious caller or received in an SMS.
faq_q3: button in the warning for, and can I trust that number?
faq_a3: When the app recognizes an organization (bank, operator, etc.), the warning shows a button with its official number — for example, 1313 for MAIB. These numbers were manually verified against official organization websites, not taken from the suspicious SMS itself. Tapping it opens the dial screen — the call isn't placed automatically, you tap
yourself once you've confirmed everything is correct.
faq_q4: Why am I not getting any warnings at all?
faq_a4: The most common reason is that not all required permissions have been granted. The home screen will show a yellow card,
, as long as at least one permission is missing. Tap it and grant everything the system asks for — especially

: without it, the yellow ring-time banner won't appear at all. If that card isn't showing and the status is green, protection is set up correctly and the issue is something else (see the next question).
faq_q5: Is it true that SMS content is never transmitted anywhere?
faq_a5: Yes. All SMS analysis happens locally on your device. The app has no technical way to send message content anywhere — the ScamGuard server does not receive or store SMS. The only network request is an anonymous confirmation of accepting the terms of use on first launch.
faq_q6: Why didn't a warning appear sometimes?
faq_a6: ScamGuard is an auxiliary tool, not a guarantee of protection. A warning appears for a specific combination of signs (code in SMS + call). New scam schemes, unusual code wording, or SMS delivery delays from the carrier may mean the app stays silent. Stay alert regardless — especially if the caller is rushing you and asking for a code.
faq_q7: Why does the app need so many permissions?
faq_a7:
• SMS reception — to analyze incoming messages for signs of fraud (on device, not sent anywhere).
• Phone state — to know if a call is currently active.
• Call management — allows the

button in the notification to actually end the call.
• Display over other apps — to show the red warning over the call screen.

Everything runs locally. SMS content is not sent anywhere.
faq_q8: I found a bug or a translation error — where do I report it?
faq_a8: Go to Settings → «Contact» section → «Report a bug» — this opens an email with the app version and device info already filled in, just describe the issue in your own words. If you noticed a translation mistake (in Russian or Romanian) — mention it in the email, this is especially valuable since the app is translated manually. There may also be a Telegram community link there, once it's set up — you can message directly there instead.
docs_subtitle: Legal information and terms of use
docs_privacy_desc: What data is processed, where it's stored and what is sent to the server.
docs_eula_desc: Conditions for installing and using the app, limitations of liability.
footer_about: Free app for monitoring phone scammers. Works locally on your device.
